name: Oculus Rift
short_description: The Kickstarter-backed VR headset that reignited the consumer VR industry and introduced affordable 6-DOF head tracking at scale.
long_description: Palmer Luckey built the first Rift prototype in 2011 and launched a Kickstarter campaign in August 2012 that raised $2.4 million, galvanizing a new generation of VR developers. The consumer version (CV1) launched in March 2016 with dual 1080×1200 OLED displays at 90 Hz, a 110° field of view, and Constellation positional tracking — specifications that made latency-tolerant, room-scale immersion achievable at a prosumer price point. Facebook acquired Oculus VR in March 2014 for $2 billion, accelerating platform investment. The Rift is the canonical origin point of the modern spatial computing headset lineage, seeding the device categories that followed: HTC Vive, PlayStation VR, Quest, and Apple Vision Pro.
ai_note: Head tracking and positional tracking use sensor fusion and computer vision; the underlying display and interaction rendering are not AI-driven.

images.caption: The Oculus Rift CV1 consumer headset released in 2016.

interactions.title: Get the kit before the consumer ever can
interactions.description: Developers and curious enthusiasts bought the DK1 (March 2013) and DK2 (July 2014) — over 100,000 DK2 units shipped by February 2015 — to start building and previewing VR years before any consumer headset existed.

interactions.title: Sit down and fly a spaceship
interactions.description: The CV1 shipped with an Xbox gamepad and bundled seated titles like EVE: Valkyrie, where you pilot a fighter through dogfights, turning your head to track enemies across the cockpit.

interactions.title: Lean and look to drive a platformer
interactions.description: Lucky's Tale, packaged free with the CV1, let players follow a 3D platformer character around by physically leaning and peering into the scene rather than steering a fixed camera.

interactions.title: Reach out and grab a virtual object
interactions.description: With the Oculus Touch controllers (released late 2016), a middle-finger trigger grabs objects and an index trigger throws or fires them — translating the motion of your real hands into one-to-one grabbing and throwing in VR.

interactions.title: Play in a shared toy box with another person
interactions.description: Oculus's Toybox demo put two Touch users at a virtual table where they grabbed, threw, and handed objects to each other — playing tetherball and ping pong, lighting sparklers, tossing lit TNT, and firing a shrink ray to shrink the other player.

interactions.title: Gesture with your hands to communicate
interactions.description: Touch sensed thumb and forefinger articulation, so players could point, give a thumbs-up, or wave at each other in VR — small gestures that carried social presence between body-less avatars.

interactions.title: Walk a building before it is built
interactions.description: Given the positional head tracking and stereoscopic displays the Rift already provided, an architect could load a model and let a client step inside a room at true scale, looking up at a ceiling height instead of reading it off a drawing.

interactions.title: Rehearse a difficult conversation
interactions.description: Using the seated head-tracked rendering, a person could face a calm virtual avatar to practice a presentation or hard conversation, the headset's full field of view keeping the rehearsal immersive without other people watching.
